stem: You are the obstetric registrar reviewing a 30-year-old G2P2 day 3 post emergency caesarean section for failure to progress at 9 cm. Her membranes were ruptured for 22 hours before delivery and she received a single dose of intravenous cefazolin at incision. Today she has a temperature of 38.6 degrees C, lower abdominal tenderness, foul-smelling lochia and uterine tenderness on bimanual examination. She is otherwise haemodynamically stable. Her FBC shows neutrophilic leucocytosis and her urine dipstick and chest examination are unremarkable.

data_table.Examination: Tender boggy uterus at umbilicus, foul-smelling lochia, CS wound clean and dry, calves soft, chest clear
data_table.Investigations: WCC 18.6 x10^9/L (neutrophils 16.0), CRP 142 mg/L, urinalysis negative, blood cultures taken, lactate 1.6 mmol/L

lead_in: What is the most appropriate antibiotic regimen?

options.text: Commence intravenous ampicillin 2 g 6-hourly plus gentamicin 5 mg/kg daily plus metronidazole 500 mg 12-hourly until clinically afebrile for 24 to 48 hours, then transition to oral amoxicillin clavulanate to complete a 7-day course
options.rationale: Therapeutic Guidelines Antibiotic recommends triple IV cover (ampicillin or amoxicillin, gentamicin and metronidazole) for postpartum endometritis to cover Gram-positive, Gram-negative and anaerobic flora until 24 to 48 hours afebrile, then step down to oral amoxicillin clavulanate. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic - Endometritis)

options.text: Commence oral cefalexin 500 mg 6-hourly plus doxycycline 100 mg 12-hourly for a 10-day outpatient course because oral options are equivalent in efficacy and avoid prolonged maternal hospital separation from the newborn
options.rationale: Postpartum endometritis with fever, leucocytosis and uterine tenderness requires inpatient IV antibiotics until afebrile; oral monotherapy delays response and increases complications. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic - Endometritis)

options.text: Administer single dose intravenous ceftriaxone 2 g and azithromycin 1 g orally as syndromic treatment for pelvic inflammatory disease and arrange outpatient gynaecology review within 7 days for clinical reassessment and follow up swabs
options.rationale: PID regimens do not adequately cover the polymicrobial pathogens of post-CS endometritis (including anaerobes); the clinical context is endometritis not classic PID. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic - Endometritis)

options.text: Treat with intravenous piperacillin tazobactam 4.5 g 8-hourly as monotherapy for 10 days regardless of clinical response, because broad-spectrum monotherapy avoids the nephrotoxicity associated with gentamicin combination regimens
options.rationale: Piperacillin tazobactam is reserved for severe sepsis or treatment failure; first-line for uncomplicated endometritis is the narrower triple-agent regimen which is effective and cheaper. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic - Endometritis)

options.text: Withhold antibiotic therapy pending blood culture and high vaginal swab results in 48 hours, and treat with intravenous paracetamol and uterotonic ergometrine while monitoring trends in inflammatory markers over the same time period
options.rationale: Withholding antibiotics in suspected endometritis with fever and leucocytosis risks progression to bacteraemia or sepsis; empirical therapy is started immediately after cultures. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic - Endometritis)

explanation.summary: Postpartum endometritis is most common after caesarean section, prolonged rupture of membranes, multiple vaginal examinations and chorioamnionitis. Clinical features include fever above 38, uterine tenderness, foul lochia and leucocytosis after the first 24 hours postpartum. Therapeutic Guidelines empirical regimen is intravenous ampicillin or amoxicillin 2 g 6-hourly, gentamicin 5 mg/kg daily and metronidazole 500 mg 12-hourly, continued until 24 to 48 hours afebrile. Step down to oral amoxicillin clavulanate to complete 7 days total in clinical responders. Re-evaluate for retained products of conception, abscess or septic pelvic thrombophlebitis if no response by 48 hours.
key_points: Postpartum endometritis is polymicrobial: aerobic and anaerobic flora
IV ampi/amoxi + gentamicin + metronidazole first-line
Step down to oral amox-clav at 24-48 hr afebrile
Total duration 7 days
If no response by 48 h, consider retained products, abscess, SPVT
explanation.pearls: Septic pelvic thrombophlebitis classically presents as persistent fever despite adequate antibiotics; treat with therapeutic anticoagulation as the cardinal diagnostic and therapeutic step.

stem: You are the antenatal care GP reviewing a 24-year-old G1P0 RhD-negative woman at 27+5 weeks. Her booking blood group was confirmed Rh-negative with no atypical antibodies on the indirect antiglobulin test, and her partner is RhD-positive. The pregnancy has been uncomplicated and she has had no episodes of bleeding, abdominal trauma or invasive procedures since conception. Her routine 28-week bloods including a repeat antibody screen have been collected today.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 116/74, HR 78, T 36.7, fundal height 28 cm
data_table.Examination: Soft non-tender abdomen, fetal heart rate 144, no bleeding
data_table.Investigations: Maternal blood group O Rh-negative, indirect antiglobulin test negative, partner Rh-positive, anti-D titre not yet performed in pregnancy

lead_in: What is the recommended schedule of anti-D immunoglobulin?

options.text: Determine fetal RhD genotype by free-fetal DNA testing at 26 weeks and administer anti-D immunoglobulin only if the fetus is confirmed RhD-positive, otherwise withhold all anti-D prophylaxis throughout the pregnancy
options.rationale: Cell-free fetal DNA-directed prophylaxis is in use in some jurisdictions but not yet standard NBA-recommended Australian practice; default is universal Rh-D negative non-immunised prophylaxis. (Source: NBA: Guidelines for the prophylactic use of Rh D immunoglobulin in obstetrics)
source_refs: NBA: Guidelines for the prophylactic use of Rh D immunoglobulin in obstetrics

options.text: Defer antenatal anti-D prophylaxis until 34 weeks because a single late-third-trimester dose has been shown to be non-inferior to the two-dose schedule and avoids the small risk of plasma-derived product transmission of infection
options.rationale: Two-dose schedule (28 and 34 weeks) is the routine Australian recommendation; single late dose is not standard practice here. (Source: NBA: Guidelines for the prophylactic use of Rh D immunoglobulin in obstetrics)
source_refs: NBA: Guidelines for the prophylactic use of Rh D immunoglobulin in obstetrics

options.text: Administer a single 1250 IU intramuscular dose now at 28 weeks and do not repeat at 34 weeks because the higher-dose single regimen provides adequate prophylaxis against subsequent fetomaternal haemorrhage until birth
options.rationale: 1250 IU is the dose used in some single-dose international regimens but Australian practice uses 625 IU at 28 and 34 weeks. (Source: NBA: Guidelines for the prophylactic use of Rh D immunoglobulin in obstetrics)
source_refs: NBA: Guidelines for the prophylactic use of Rh D immunoglobulin in obstetrics

options.text: Withhold antenatal anti-D prophylaxis and administer only a postnatal dose within 72 hours of birth if the baby tests RhD-positive on cord blood sampling at delivery, as routine antenatal dosing is no longer recommended in low-risk pregnancies
options.rationale: Antenatal prophylaxis significantly reduces alloimmunisation; postnatal-only dosing is outdated practice and not endorsed by NBA. (Source: NBA: Guidelines for the prophylactic use of Rh D immunoglobulin in obstetrics)
source_refs: NBA: Guidelines for the prophylactic use of Rh D immunoglobulin in obstetrics

options.text: Administer 625 IU of intramuscular anti-D immunoglobulin at 28 weeks and again at 34 weeks, with a further dose within 72 hours of birth if the baby is RhD-positive on cord blood testing
options.rationale: Australian National Blood Authority guidance for RhD-negative women without anti-D antibodies is routine antenatal prophylaxis at 28 and 34 weeks (625 IU IM each) plus postnatal dose if baby is Rh-positive, plus event-driven doses. (Source: NBA: Guidelines for the prophylactic use of Rh D immunoglobulin in obstetrics)
source_refs: NBA: Guidelines for the prophylactic use of Rh D immunoglobulin in obstetrics

explanation.summary: Australian National Blood Authority guidance for routine antenatal anti-D prophylaxis in RhD-negative non-immunised women is 625 IU of anti-D immunoglobulin intramuscularly at 28 weeks AND at 34 weeks. A further 625 IU is given within 72 hours of birth if the baby is RhD-positive (cord blood test). Event-driven doses are added for any sensitising event: miscarriage or termination at 12+ weeks (250 IU before 12 weeks, 625 IU at or after), ectopic pregnancy, antepartum haemorrhage, abdominal trauma, external cephalic version, amniocentesis, chorionic villus sampling. Kleihauer-Betke test quantifies fetomaternal haemorrhage and guides additional dosing.
key_points: 625 IU IM at 28 weeks AND 34 weeks (routine antenatal)
625 IU IM within 72 h of birth if baby Rh-positive
Event-driven doses for sensitising events
Kleihauer-Betke for FMH quantification
Confirm by indirect antiglobulin test for ongoing antibody screening
explanation.pearls: If anti-D is missed within 72 hours, give up to 9 to 10 days post-event - residual benefit persists even beyond the 72-hour window.

sources.label: NBA: Guidelines for the prophylactic use of Rh D immunoglobulin in obstetrics

subtopic_detail: Severe pre-eclampsia at 32 weeks - magnesium and steroid timing
stem: You are the obstetric registrar admitting a 27-year-old G1P0 at 32+1 weeks who presents to triage with new severe headache, blurred vision and right upper quadrant pain. Her BP was 158/108 on arrival and a repeat at 15 minutes is 162/110. She has 3+ proteinuria on dipstick and brisk lower limb reflexes with no clonus. The CTG shows a baseline of 140 with normal variability and no decelerations. The fetus is in cephalic presentation, ultrasound at 30 weeks showed normal growth and Dopplers.

data_table.Examination: Brisk reflexes no clonus, no focal neurology, soft non-tender abdomen, fundal height 31 cm, CTG reassuring
data_table.Investigations: Urine PCR 380 mg/mmol, platelets 138 x10^9/L, ALT 92 U/L, AST 88 U/L, creatinine 78 micromol/L, Hb 124 g/L, INR 1.0

lead_in: What is the most appropriate immediate management plan?

options.text: Commence a low-dose aspirin 150 mg daily and calcium 1.5 g daily as secondary prevention of pre-eclampsia and arrange a fortnightly outpatient growth and Doppler scan and growth scans aiming for delivery at term in this primigravida
options.rationale: Aspirin and calcium are primary prevention strategies started early in pregnancy; once severe pre-eclampsia has developed at 32 weeks these are not appropriate as a substitute for acute stabilisation and planned delivery. (Source: SOMANZ: Hypertensive Disorders of Pregnancy 2023)

options.text: Commence oral methyldopa 250 mg three times daily and arrange weekly outpatient antenatal review with home blood pressure monitoring, deferring magnesium and steroids until 34 weeks because expectant management is preferred at this gestation
options.rationale: Severe-range blood pressure with neurological symptoms and end-organ involvement excludes outpatient management; methyldopa is not used acutely for severe pre-eclampsia. (Source: SOMANZ: Hypertensive Disorders of Pregnancy 2023)

options.text: Deliver immediately by category 1 caesarean section regardless of cervical state because severe pre-eclampsia with any HELLP feature is an absolute contraindication to attempted vaginal birth at any gestation under 37 weeks
options.rationale: Severe pre-eclampsia is not an absolute indication for category 1 caesarean unless there is fetal compromise or eclampsia not controlled by magnesium; planned delivery (vaginal or CS) after stabilisation is appropriate. (Source: SOMANZ: Hypertensive Disorders of Pregnancy 2023)

options.text: Administer intravenous hydralazine 10 mg bolus and intravenous furosemide 40 mg now, omit magnesium because reflexes show no clonus, and arrange induction of labour over the next 12 hours regardless of fetal lung maturity at this gestation
options.rationale: Furosemide is not used unless pulmonary oedema is established; magnesium is indicated for severe pre-eclampsia regardless of clonus to prevent seizures; steroids should be given for fetal lung maturity at this gestation. (Source: SOMANZ: Hypertensive Disorders of Pregnancy 2023)

options.text: Administer intravenous magnesium sulfate 4 g loading then 1 g/hour for seizure prophylaxis, treat her blood pressure with oral or intravenous labetalol or nifedipine to target under 160/110, give intramuscular betamethasone 11.4 mg and plan delivery within 24 to 48 hours
options.rationale: Severe pre-eclampsia (BP at or above 160/110 plus end-organ involvement) at 32 weeks requires magnesium for seizure prophylaxis, urgent BP control, antenatal corticosteroids for fetal lung maturity, and planned delivery after stabilisation typically within 24 to 48 hours. (Source: SOMANZ: Hypertensive Disorders of Pregnancy 2023)

explanation.summary: Severe pre-eclampsia is defined by systolic BP at or above 160 or diastolic at or above 110 mmHg, persistent neurological symptoms, severe right upper quadrant or epigastric pain with raised transaminases, pulmonary oedema, oliguria, thrombocytopenia under 100, or fetal compromise. Management at any gestation includes intravenous magnesium sulfate (4 g loading over 20 minutes then 1 g/hour for 24 hours postnatally) for seizure prophylaxis, rapid BP control with labetalol, hydralazine or oral nifedipine targeting under 160/110, antenatal corticosteroids if under 34+6 weeks for lung maturity, and planned delivery after maternal stabilisation (typically within 24 to 48 hours at 32 weeks).
key_points: Severe BP threshold 160/110 with end-organ features
Mg sulfate 4 g IV load then 1 g/hr for seizure prophylaxis and ongoing 24 h postnatal
BP control: IV labetalol, hydralazine or oral nifedipine to under 160/110
Betamethasone if under 34+6 weeks
Delivery after stabilisation, typically within 24-48 h
explanation.pearls: Magnesium toxicity is monitored by hourly checks of patellar reflex, respiratory rate (over 12) and urine output (over 30 mL/h); IV calcium gluconate 10 mL of 10% is the antidote.

stem: You are the obstetric registrar called to triage to assess a 33-year-old G3P2 at 34+2 weeks who has had a sudden painless bright red vaginal bleed of approximately 200 mL while resting at home this evening. Her 20-week morphology scan identified a complete placenta praevia covering the internal os; a follow-up scan at 32 weeks confirmed the placenta still covered the os. Her previous two deliveries were by caesarean section. She is anxious but haemodynamically stable. The CTG is reassuring with no contractions felt.

data_table.Examination: Abdomen soft non-tender, fundal height 34 cm, cephalic presentation, sanitary pad shows fresh blood approximately 50 mL since arrival, CTG baseline 140 normal variability
data_table.Investigations: Hb 114 g/L, platelets 220 x10^9/L, group and screen, crossmatch in progress, INR 1.0, fibrinogen 4.1 g/L

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Reassure her and discharge home with strict bed rest and instructions to return with any further bleeding, contractions or reduced fetal movements over the coming days until the planned 36-week elective caesarean delivery
options.rationale: Discharge after a 200 mL bleed in known praevia is unsafe due to risk of further haemorrhage; inpatient observation is mandated until delivery in most cases. (Source: RANZCOG: Placenta praevia, accreta and vasa praevia)
source_refs: RANZCOG: Placenta praevia, accreta and vasa praevia

options.text: Perform a digital vaginal examination to assess cervical dilatation and the lower uterine segment placental relationship and proceed to artificial rupture of membranes if the cervix is favourable to induce labour for vaginal birth
options.rationale: Digital examination is contraindicated in known praevia because it can provoke catastrophic haemorrhage; vaginal birth is not planned in complete praevia. (Source: RANZCOG: Placenta praevia, accreta and vasa praevia)
source_refs: RANZCOG: Placenta praevia, accreta and vasa praevia

options.text: Admit for inpatient observation with continuous CTG, two large-bore intravenous cannulas, crossmatched blood ready, intramuscular betamethasone for fetal lung maturity, no vaginal examination, and discuss timing of caesarean delivery with the consultant
options.rationale: A symptomatic praevia at 34 weeks with a 200 mL bleed mandates inpatient admission, IV access, crossmatched blood available, antenatal corticosteroids if not previously given, strict avoidance of vaginal examination, and consultant-level planning for elective caesarean (typically at 36 to 37 weeks if stable). (Source: RANZCOG: Placenta praevia, accreta and vasa praevia)
source_refs: RANZCOG: Placenta praevia, accreta and vasa praevia

options.text: Proceed immediately to category 1 caesarean section without waiting for crossmatched blood or maternal stabilisation because any antepartum haemorrhage at over 32 weeks in placenta praevia mandates immediate delivery regardless of CTG
options.rationale: Category 1 delivery is reserved for ongoing maternal haemodynamic compromise, fetal compromise or massive haemorrhage; the woman is stable so stabilisation and planned delivery is appropriate. (Source: RANZCOG: Placenta praevia, accreta and vasa praevia)
source_refs: RANZCOG: Placenta praevia, accreta and vasa praevia

options.text: Administer tocolytic indomethacin and intravenous tranexamic acid 1 g and continue outpatient management with a daily home CTG and weekly hospital review for ongoing bleeding episodes until 37 weeks of gestation are reached
options.rationale: Indomethacin tocolysis is not used at 34 weeks for praevia bleeding and ongoing outpatient management after a significant bleed is unsafe. (Source: RANZCOG: Placenta praevia, accreta and vasa praevia)
source_refs: RANZCOG: Placenta praevia, accreta and vasa praevia

explanation.summary: Placenta praevia is the abnormal implantation of the placenta over or near the internal cervical os and is a leading cause of painless antepartum haemorrhage. Major praevia (covering os) is delivered by elective caesarean at 36+0 to 37+6 weeks if stable. Symptomatic bleeding mandates admission, two large-bore IV cannulas, FBC, group and hold (or crossmatch if significant), Kleihauer if Rh-negative, anti-D, antenatal corticosteroids if under 34+6 weeks, and strict avoidance of vaginal examination. Previous caesarean increases risk of placenta accreta spectrum, which should be assessed by MRI or detailed ultrasound at 32 to 34 weeks before delivery planning.
key_points: Painless bright PV bleed = praevia until proven otherwise
Never perform digital VE in known praevia
Admit, large-bore IV, crossmatch, betamethasone if under 34+6
Plan elective CS 36+0 to 37+6 weeks
Prior CS plus anterior praevia = accreta spectrum risk (MRI/US workup)
explanation.pearls: Vasa praevia is a separate entity with painless PV bleed at membrane rupture - small volume but rapid fetal exsanguination; high index of suspicion if velamentous cord insertion noted antenatally.

sources.label: RANZCOG: Placenta praevia, accreta and vasa praevia
RCOG: Placenta praevia, placenta accreta and vasa praevia (Green-top 27a)

stem: You are the obstetric registrar called urgently to the labour ward where a 26-year-old G2P1 at 38+4 weeks has just had spontaneous rupture of membranes during a routine examination. She was at 4 cm cervical dilatation with the head at -3 station. The midwife felt a pulsating cord at the introitus on the gush of liquor. The CTG had been reassuring but now shows a prolonged bradycardia of 70 bpm sustained for 2 minutes. The mother is supine on the bed. The theatre team has not been activated yet.

data_table.Vital signs: Mother: BP 124/76, HR 102, SpO2 99%, T 36.8; Fetal: HR 70 sustained 2 minutes
data_table.Examination: Cord palpable and pulsating at introitus, cervix 4 cm, head at -3 station, baseline preceding was 140
data_table.Investigations: Recent FBC and group and screen routine, no abnormalities

lead_in: What is the most appropriate immediate action?

options.text: Encourage rapid pushing with sustained Valsalva manoeuvres and prepare for an operative vaginal birth with rotational Kielland forceps because vaginal birth at 4 cm with a sustained bradycardia is the fastest route to delivery
options.rationale: At 4 cm dilatation vaginal birth is not imminent and pushing is not effective; rotational forceps in this scenario is unsafe. CS is the appropriate route. (Source: RANZCOG: Umbilical cord prolapse)

options.text: Place the mother in knee-chest position or steep Trendelenburg with manual elevation of the presenting part to relieve cord compression, activate category 1 caesarean section and consider tocolysis with terbutaline 250 mcg subcutaneously en route to theatre
options.rationale: Immediate cord decompression by knee-chest/Trendelenburg with manual elevation, plus activation of category 1 CS (within 30 minutes) and bridging tocolysis with terbutaline 250 mcg SC, is the RANZCOG standard for cord prolapse with fetal compromise. (Source: RANZCOG: Umbilical cord prolapse)

options.text: Reduce the cord back into the uterus manually and place a tight perineal pad over the introitus to prevent further prolapse, then continue routine labour management with intermittent auscultation over the following hours
options.rationale: Attempting to replace the cord risks further trauma and does not address the underlying compression; definitive delivery is required. (Source: RANZCOG: Umbilical cord prolapse)

options.text: Administer intravenous oxytocin 5 IU bolus followed by an infusion to augment contractions and accelerate cervical dilatation to enable vaginal birth, and reassess the CTG after 15 minutes of augmented contractions
options.rationale: Augmentation will worsen cord compression by increasing the strength of contractions against the cord; tocolysis (the opposite) is preferred. (Source: RANZCOG: Umbilical cord prolapse)

options.text: Insert a 500 mL Foley catheter into the bladder and inflate the balloon to elevate the presenting part, then transfer the patient by ambulance to a tertiary maternal-fetal medicine centre for definitive operative delivery
options.rationale: Bladder filling is a recognised adjunct for transfer over longer distances but immediate operative delivery on-site is preferred when theatre is available; transfer delays definitive care. (Source: RANZCOG: Umbilical cord prolapse)

explanation.summary: Umbilical cord prolapse is an obstetric emergency in which the cord descends below the presenting part after membrane rupture, leading to compression and fetal hypoxia. Immediate management has three pillars: relieve cord compression, monitor fetus, and expedite delivery. Compression relief by knee-chest or steep Trendelenburg with manual elevation of the presenting part by the examining hand kept in the vagina, plus filling the bladder with 500 mL saline via Foley if transfer is required. Tocolysis with subcutaneous terbutaline 250 mcg buys time. Definitive treatment is category 1 caesarean section unless full dilatation with imminent vaginal birth allows safer assisted vaginal delivery.
key_points: Knee-chest position or steep Trendelenburg
Manual elevation of presenting part in vagina until delivery
Tocolysis with terbutaline 250 mcg SC
Category 1 CS (decision-to-delivery 30 min)
Bladder filling 500 mL for transfer if delay anticipated
explanation.pearls: Avoid handling or warming the prolapsed cord beyond gentle replacement - vasospasm worsens fetal hypoxia and aggressive manipulation increases the risk of cord rupture.
